description: FALSE-POSITIVE BAIT — coach-articulated. The coach offers an interpretive framing; the client agrees passively ('yeah, that's it', 'I think that's right'). The v7 prompt says: 'The shift has to come from the client's own words.' Expected: themes captured at moderate intensity (4-6), 0 shifts (the moment-level realization came from the COACH, not the client), 0 breakthroughs. If v7 attributes the shift to the client when the client only echoed the coach's framing, the rubric is failing the 'client's own words' rule.

messages.content: Good to see you. What's most alive for you today?
I had this thing with my partner. He didn't unload the dishwasher and I went off on him and now I feel terrible. It was a small thing. I overreacted.
What was happening in you right before you went off?
I'd had a long day. A bad meeting. I came home and saw the dishwasher and just snapped.
It sounds like the dishwasher wasn't really about the dishwasher. The bad meeting was looking for somewhere to land, and the dishwasher was the closest target.
I wonder if you're carrying more from work than you realize, and it's coming out sideways at the people closest to you.
Yeah, that makes sense. I think I do that.
That seems like a pattern worth noticing — work stress doesn't get expressed at work, so it leaks into your relationships at home.
Mm-hm. Yeah. That's kind of what I do.
Maybe one thing to try this week is checking in with yourself before you walk in the door — what am I bringing in with me?
No, I think that's it. Thanks. That was helpful.
